summary: The author developed a violation detection solution by augmenting the training dataset with rule-specific paraphrases generated using Qwen3-32B. They fine-tuned three instruction-tuned LLMs (Phi-4, Qwen-2.5-14B-Instruct, and Qwen3-14B) via QLoRA to predict binary rule violations based on input text and target rules. Training was accelerated with Unsloth and Distributed Data Parallel across multiple GPUs. The approach prioritized targeted, rule-aware data augmentation and efficient parameter-efficient fine-tuning over more complex strategies like pseudo-labeling or alternative optimizers.
extensive_summary:
## Data Augmentation via Rule-Specific Paraphrasing
The core innovation of this solution lies in its targeted data augmentation strategy. Rather than applying generic text transformations, the author leveraged Qwen3-32B to generate one paraphrased variant per original training sample for each violation rule. Custom prompts were carefully crafted to preserve either the violating or compliant nature of the text while altering its wording. This rule-aware augmentation was designed to improve linguistic diversity and model robustness across distinct violation categories such as financial advice, medical advice, illegal activity, spoilers, and advertising.

## Model Training and Architecture
Three large language models were fine-tuned: Phi-4, Qwen-2.5-14B-Instruct, and Qwen3-14B. Each model was configured to accept both the comment `body` and the target `rule` as input, framing the task as a binary classification problem (outputting "Yes" or "No" for rule violation). To make training feasible on limited hardware, QLoRA was employed. The training pipeline utilized Unsloth for memory-efficient optimization and Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) to scale computation across multiple GPUs.

## Lessons Learned
The author explicitly documents several techniques that failed to improve performance. Pseudo-labeling, the Muon optimizer, and generating new training data via few-shot prompting all yielded no gains. This underscores that for this specific task formulation, sticking to targeted, rule-aware augmentation and efficient fine-tuning is more effective than experimenting with untested optimizers or self-training strategies.
competition_flow: Raw text data was augmented with rule-specific paraphrases generated by Qwen3-32B, then fed into three LLMs fine-tuned via QLoRA and Unsloth DDP to predict binary rule violations, with predictions submitted directly.
data_processing: Rule-specific paraphrasing of training data using Qwen3-32B to generate one augmented sample per original body per rule
insights: Targeted, rule-aware data augmentation is more effective than generic expansion or pseudo-labeling for violation detection tasks.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) combined with memory optimization (Unsloth) enables training multiple large models efficiently.
critical_findings: Pseudo-labeling, the Muon optimizer, and few-shot prompting for data generation all failed to improve performance, suggesting they are unsuitable for this specific data distribution or task formulation.
what_did_not_work: new training data using few-shot prompting
